questions.question: A fair 6-sided die is rolled. What is the expected value of the face shown?

explanations.B: 6 is the maximum face, not the average.
explanations.C: 1 is the minimum face, not the average.
explanations.D: 3 forgets the +1: the mean of 1..6 is (6+1)/2.

questions.question: A contract pays $10 if a fair coin lands heads and $0 if tails. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: Correct. EV = ½·10 + ½·0 = 5.
explanations.B: $10 is the larger payout (the max), not the average.
explanations.C: $0 is the smaller payout, a lower bound, not the average.
explanations.D: $2.5 is a quarter of the total, not half.

questions.question: You make a market on the sum of two fair six-sided dice. What fair value should center your quote?

explanations.A: 12 is the maximum sum (all sixes), not the average.
explanations.B: 2 is the minimum sum (all ones), not the average.
explanations.C: Correct. E[sum] = 2 × 3.5 = 7.
explanations.D: 3.5 is one die's mean — you forgot to add across all the dice.

questions.question: 2 fair coins are flipped and a contract pays $1 for each head. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: Correct. E[heads] = 2 × 0.5 = 1, so EV = $1.
explanations.B: $2 is the payout if every coin is heads (the max), not the expectation.
explanations.C: $0.50 is a single coin's expected heads, not all of them.
explanations.D: $4 doubles the count — there are only 2 coins.

questions.question: A contract pays $60 if a fair die shows a 6 (probability 0.1667), and $0 otherwise. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: $60 is the full payout and ignores the probability weighting.
explanations.B: 0.1667 is the probability, not the dollar EV.
explanations.C: Correct. EV = p × payoff = 0.1667 × 60 = 10.
explanations.D: $0 is the losing outcome, but the win case has positive probability.

questions.question: A card is drawn from a standard deck and pays its rank in dollars (Ace=$1, …, King=$13). What is its fair value?

explanations.A: $13 is the king (max rank), not the average.
explanations.B: $6.5 averages 0..13; ranks run 1..13, giving 7.
explanations.C: Correct. Ranks 1..13 are equally likely, so E = (1+13)/2 = 7.
explanations.D: $26 has no basis as a probability-weighted average.

questions.question: A card pays its blackjack value: Ace=$1, 2–10 at face, and J/Q/K=$10. What is the fair value of one draw?

explanations.A: $7 treats all 13 ranks as 1..13; here J/Q/K cap at 10, lowering the mean.
explanations.B: $10 is the max card value, not the average.
explanations.C: $6.5 is a rounded guess, not the exact 85/13.
explanations.D: Correct. Per suit values sum to 1+2+…+10+10+10+10 = 85 over 13 cards, so E = 85/13 ≈ 6.5385.

questions.question: Fair value is 7 and you want a spread of 2 centered on fair. What is your two-sided quote (bid at ask)?

explanations.A: Correct. Midpoint 7, half-spread 1 → 6 at 8.
explanations.B: That midpoint is 8, above fair — not centered.
explanations.C: That midpoint is 6, below fair — not centered.
explanations.D: A zero-width quote leaves you no spread to earn.

explanations.C: 2 is just one mixed outcome, not the probability-weighted mean.

questions.question: A spinner lands uniformly on the interval [0, 10] and pays that number in dollars. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: $10 is the maximum, not the average of a uniform interval.
explanations.B: $0 is the minimum, not the average.
explanations.C: Correct. For a uniform [0, 10], E = 10/2 = 5.
explanations.D: $2.5 is a quarter of the range — the uniform mean is the midpoint.

explanations.D: Correct. For a uniform [0, 100], E = 100/2 = 50.

questions.question: What is the expected number of rolls of a fair die until the first 6?

explanations.A: 0.1667 is the success probability, not the expected count (which is 1/p).
explanations.B: Correct. For a geometric trial, E = 1/p = 1/0.1667 = 6.
explanations.C: 1 would be the count only if success were certain on the first try.
explanations.D: 36 squares the answer; the expectation is 1/p.

questions.question: What is the expected number of flips of a fair coin until the first head?

explanations.A: 0.5 is the success probability, not the expected count (which is 1/p).
explanations.B: 1 would be the count only if success were certain on the first try.
explanations.C: Correct. For a geometric trial, E = 1/p = 1/0.5 = 2.
explanations.D: 4 squares the answer; the expectation is 1/p.

questions.question: What is the expected number of rolls of a fair die until you see a 1 or 2?

explanations.A: 0.3333 is the success probability, not the expected count (which is 1/p).
explanations.B: 1 would be the count only if success were certain on the first try.
explanations.C: 9 squares the answer; the expectation is 1/p.
explanations.D: Correct. For a geometric trial, E = 1/p = 1/0.3333 = 3.

questions.question: What is the expected number of draws (with replacement) from a deck until the first ace?

explanations.A: Correct. For a geometric trial, E = 1/p = 1/0.0769 = 13.
explanations.B: 0.0769 is the success probability, not the expected count (which is 1/p).
explanations.C: 1 would be the count only if success were certain on the first try.
explanations.D: 169 squares the answer; the expectation is 1/p.

questions.question: What is the expected number of rolls of a fair die until you see a number greater than 4?

explanations.A: 0.3333 is the success probability, not the expected count (which is 1/p).
explanations.B: Correct. For a geometric trial, E = 1/p = 1/0.3333 = 3.
explanations.C: 1 would be the count only if success were certain on the first try.
explanations.D: 9 squares the answer; the expectation is 1/p.

questions.question: What is the expected number of spins until a 1-in-10 jackpot sector hits?

explanations.A: 0.1 is the success probability, not the expected count (which is 1/p).
explanations.B: 1 would be the count only if success were certain on the first try.
explanations.C: Correct. For a geometric trial, E = 1/p = 1/0.1 = 10.
explanations.D: 100 squares the answer; the expectation is 1/p.

questions.question: Two fair six-sided dice are rolled and the contract pays the larger of the two faces (the max). What is its fair value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is one die's mean; the max is biased upward, above 3.5.
explanations.B: 6 is the largest possible max, not the average max.
explanations.C: 2.5278 is E[min], the expected smaller die, not the max.
explanations.D: Correct. E[max] = Σ k·P(max=k) = 161/36 ≈ 4.4722.

questions.question: Two fair six-sided dice are rolled and the contract pays the smaller of the two faces (the min). What is its fair value?

explanations.A: Correct. E[min] = 91/36 ≈ 2.5278 (and E[max]+E[min] = 7).
explanations.B: 3.5 is one die's mean; the min is biased below 3.5.
explanations.C: 1 is the smallest possible min, not the average min.
explanations.D: 4.4722 is E[max], not the min.

questions.question: Two fair six-sided dice are rolled and the contract pays the absolute difference |X − Y|. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: 0 is E[X−Y] (signed difference); the absolute value is positive on average.
explanations.C: 3.5 is a single die mean, unrelated to the spread between two dice.
explanations.D: 5 is the maximum possible difference (6−1), not the average.

questions.question: A fair six-sided die is rolled and the contract pays the square of the face. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: 12.25 is (E[X])² = 3.5²; E[X²] is larger because of variance.
explanations.B: 36 is the maximum square (6²), not the average.
explanations.D: 3.5 is E[X], not E[X²].

questions.question: Your two-sided quote's midpoint sits below true fair value. What does informed flow most likely do?
options: Hit your bid, because it is rich versus fair
Lift your ask, because it is cheap versus fair

explanations.A: They sell only if your bid is ABOVE fair; here the whole quote is low.
explanations.B: A mispriced quote attracts MORE informed flow, not less.
explanations.C: Informed flow trades the profitable side, not randomly.
explanations.D: Correct. An ask below fair is underpriced, so informed buyers buy from you.

questions.question: You are long a large position and want to reduce inventory risk. How should you skew your quote around fair value?
options: Lower both bid and ask (skew down) to encourage selling and discourage buying
Raise both bid and ask (skew up)
Keep the quote exactly centered on fair

explanations.A: Correct. Skewing down makes your bid less attractive to sellers and your ask more attractive to buyers, so you shed the long.
explanations.B: Skewing up invites more buying from you, increasing your long position.
explanations.C: Widening reduces flow but does not preferentially shed your long inventory.
explanations.D: A centered quote ignores your inventory risk.

questions.question: Fair value is 7. You quote 7 at 9. Where is your edge weakest?
options: On the ask: 9 is too far above fair
On the bid: at 7 (= fair) you earn nothing when someone sells to you
The spread of 2 is too wide
Nowhere — the quote is centered on fair

explanations.A: An ask above fair is the profitable side, not the weak one.
explanations.B: Correct. A bid equal to fair captures no margin; all your spread sits on the ask side.
explanations.C: Spread width is not the issue; the quote is simply not centered.
explanations.D: Midpoint is 8, not 7, so it is skewed high.

questions.question: Fair value of a contract is $4. A counterparty offers to sell it to you at $3. As a rational market maker, you should:
options: Decline — $3 is below fair so it must be a trap
Buy it — paying $3 for something worth $4 is +$1 EV
Wait until the price rises to $4

explanations.A: Buying BELOW fair is good for you; cheap is exactly what you want.
explanations.B: Selling at $3 something worth $4 loses you $1.
explanations.C: Correct. Price $3 < fair $4, so buying captures $1 of expected edge.
explanations.D: Waiting forgoes a guaranteed positive-EV trade now.

questions.question: Fair value is 100. You are flat (no inventory) and have no information edge. The textbook symmetric quote with spread 4 is:

explanations.A: Midpoint 102 skews the quote above fair.
explanations.B: Midpoint 98 skews the quote below fair.
explanations.C: Midpoint 101 is still above fair, not symmetric.
explanations.D: Correct. Symmetric around 100 with width 4: 100 ± 2 = 98 at 102.

questions.question: A contract pays the sum of two fair dice plus a fixed $5 bonus. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: Correct. E[sum] = 7, plus the certain $5 → 7 + 5 = 12.
explanations.B: $7 is the dice sum EV but omits the certain $5 bonus.
explanations.C: $5 is only the bonus and omits the dice sum EV of 7.
explanations.D: $17 adds the $5 to the max sum 12 instead of the EV 7.

questions.question: A contract pays twice the sum of two fair dice. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: $7 forgets to apply the ×2 multiplier.
explanations.B: Correct. 2 × E[sum] = 2 × 7 = 14.
explanations.C: $24 is twice the maximum sum (12), not twice the EV.
explanations.D: $28 doubles 14 — one extra factor of 2.

questions.question: A biased coin pays $1 on heads with P(heads)=0.6, plus you collect a certain $2 fee. What total fair value centers your market?

explanations.A: $0.60 is the coin EV only and omits the certain $2 fee.
explanations.B: $1 assumes a fair coin and ignores the fee.
explanations.C: Correct. Coin EV = 0.6×1 = 0.6, plus the certain $2 → 2.6.
explanations.D: $3 overstates — it adds the fee to a full $1 payout, not the EV.

questions.question: You roll one fair die. If it is even you win its value in dollars; if odd you win $0. What is the fair value?

explanations.A: $3.50 is the unconditional die mean, but odd rolls pay $0.
explanations.B: $4 is the average of the even faces only, not weighted by the 1/2 chance of being even.
explanations.C: $6 is the max even face, not the EV.
explanations.D: Correct. Even faces 2,4,6 each prob 1/6: (2+4+6)/6 = 12/6 = 2.

questions.question: Two fair coins are flipped. The contract pays $4 only if both land heads, else $0. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: Correct. P(HH) = 1/4, so EV = (1/4)·4 = 1.
explanations.B: $4 ignores that both-heads happens only 1/4 of the time.
explanations.C: $2 assumes a 1/2 chance, but P(both heads)=1/4.
explanations.D: $0.50 uses 1/8; two coins give P(HH)=1/4.

questions.question: A contract pays $8 if at least one of two fair coins is heads, else $0. What is its fair value?

explanations.A: $8 ignores the 1/4 chance of zero heads.
explanations.B: Correct. P(at least one head) = 1 − 1/4 = 3/4, so EV = (3/4)·8 = 6.
explanations.C: $4 uses probability 1/2 instead of 3/4.
explanations.D: $2 uses 1/4, the probability of NO heads, not at least one.

questions.question: A die is rolled. You learn only that it is greater than 3 (i.e., 4, 5, or 6). The contract pays the face in dollars. What is the conditional fair value?

explanations.A: $3.50 is the unconditional mean, ignoring the information that it exceeds 3.
explanations.B: $6 is the max of the conditioned set, not its mean.
explanations.C: Correct. Given {4,5,6} equally likely, E = (4+5+6)/3 = 5.
explanations.D: $4.50 is the midpoint of 3 and 6, not the mean of {4,5,6}.

questions.question: Fair value is 7. You buy the contract at 6. What is your expected edge per contract?

explanations.A: $0 would require trading exactly at fair value.
explanations.B: $-1 has the sign backwards — this trade is profitable, not a loss.
explanations.C: $7 is the fair value itself, not the edge over the traded price.
explanations.D: Correct. Edge = fair − price = 7 − 6 = 1.

questions.question: Fair value is 7. You sell the contract at 8. What is your expected edge per contract?

explanations.A: Correct. Edge = price − fair = 8 − 7 = 1.
explanations.B: $0 would require trading exactly at fair value.
explanations.C: $-1 has the sign backwards — this trade is profitable, not a loss.
explanations.D: $7 is the fair value itself, not the edge over the traded price.

questions.question: Fair value is 12.25. You buy the contract at 12. What is your expected edge per contract?

explanations.A: $0 would require trading exactly at fair value.
explanations.B: Correct. Edge = fair − price = 12.25 − 12 = 0.25.
explanations.C: $-0.25 has the sign backwards — this trade is profitable, not a loss.
explanations.D: $12.25 is the fair value itself, not the edge over the traded price.

questions.question: Fair value is 50. You buy the contract at 47. What is your expected edge per contract?

explanations.A: $0 would require trading exactly at fair value.
explanations.B: $-3 has the sign backwards — this trade is profitable, not a loss.
explanations.C: Correct. Edge = fair − price = 50 − 47 = 3.
explanations.D: $50 is the fair value itself, not the edge over the traded price.

questions.question: Fair value is 20. You sell the contract at 23. What is your expected edge per contract?

explanations.A: $0 would require trading exactly at fair value.
explanations.B: $-3 has the sign backwards — this trade is profitable, not a loss.
explanations.C: $20 is the fair value itself, not the edge over the traded price.
explanations.D: Correct. Edge = price − fair = 23 − 20 = 3.

questions.question: Fair value is 100. You buy the contract at 95. What is your expected edge per contract?

explanations.A: Correct. Edge = fair − price = 100 − 95 = 5.
explanations.B: $0 would require trading exactly at fair value.
explanations.C: $-5 has the sign backwards — this trade is profitable, not a loss.
explanations.D: $100 is the fair value itself, not the edge over the traded price.
